purpose: Reframes QT from a quantity-of-money question to a portfolio composition question, using sectoral balance accounting identities to clarify what QT actually does
components: QT changes the composition of private sector portfolios (more duration, fewer reserves), not just quantity of money
Sectoral balance identity: government deficit = private sector surplus + current account deficit
Duration supply from QT IS the accounting mirror of fiscal deficits creating private sector financial assets — same flow, different ledger entries
The compound duration absorption problem is therefore a pricing question (at what term premium will portfolios rebalance?), not a solvency question
Net private sector financial position improves with government deficits — the bonds being absorbed are simultaneously the sector's wealth
current_assessment: Provides the correct analytical framing for understanding compound duration absorption. The double-entry insight (duration supply = fiscal deficit = private sector income) is non-obvious and underutilized in market commentary. Reframes narrative from 'who will buy all these bonds' to 'at what price will portfolios rebalance.' Currently the most sophisticated analytical frame for thinking about the QT-fiscal interaction.
limitations: Accounting identities guarantee consistency but not equilibrium pricing levels or transition dynamics
Does not predict at what term premium level the market clears
Aggregate identity does not address distributional effects — who holds the duration matters for stability
Portfolio rebalancing friction and leverage amplification are outside the framework's scope
Framework is unfamiliar to most market participants, limiting its communicative utility
